data.title: Empire Company Limited 2025 Q4 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Empire Company Limited in conjunction with their 2025 Q4 earnings call.

data.title: CoreWeave's Squeeze Isn't Over Yet
data.description:
CoreWeave’s (NASDAQ:CRWV) 340% surge since its March 27 IPO, fueled by AI enthusiasm and a violent short squeeze, has turned heads across Wall Street. Our bullish call delivered a 289% return since my first bullish call and 58% since the second.

But as borrow cost by short sellers decrease from its 343% peak and short interest stays high around 32%, the narrative is shifting from temporary technical catalysts to long-term fundamentals.

Investors should closely monitor two key dates: the early-August release of the Q2 earnings, which could trigger the early sales of lock-up shares, and the end of the full 180-day lock-up in late September.

Lock-Up Expiration and Insider Supply Overhang

A major consideration for CoreWeave is the impending expiration of IPO lock-up agreements. Approximately 83.7% of outstanding shares (held by directors, executives, pre-IPO investors, etc.) are subject to a 180-day lock-up from the March 27, 2025 prospectus date, with an early release trigger shortly after the company’s Q2 2025 earnings announcement.

In practical terms, the standard lock-up is set to lapse around late September 2025 (or two trading days after Q2 earnings, if sooner). Notably, CoreWeave’s lock-up terms include a provision that if the stock trades 25% above the $40 IPO price (>$50) for at least 5 out of 10 days after the first post-IPO earnings release (Q1 2025), roughly 1.0 million employee-held shares can be sold immediately. Given the stock indeed surpassed $50 in April, a small portion of insider shares likely became eligible for sale early.

The full lock-up expiration in Q3 poses a significant supply risk. Early investors and insiders who have seen multi-fold gains may look to realize profits, potentially flooding the market with shares. CoreWeave’s S-1 explicitly warns that when lock-up restrictions end, a wave of insider selling (or even the perception of it) could pressure the stock price.

The IPO included only ~37.5 million new Class A shares (plus ~0.9 million from a few selling stockholders, meaning the free float has so far been relatively limited. Post-September, hundreds of millions of currently-restricted shares (including those held by co-founders and venture backers) will become eligible to trade.

This time frame is key for investors: all CoreWeave holds must factor in volatility risk at the lock-up expiry date since insider selling may temporarily depress the stock price and drive trading volume sharply higher. Conversely, the lock-up expiry should enhance liquidity and new institutional buying but probably at the expense of weak short-term price action.

Short Interest and Technical Sentiment

CoreWeave’s meteoric rise has been exacerbated by extraordinarily high short interest and the potential for a short squeeze. In the weeks after the IPO, short sellers piled into the stock, perhaps questioning its rich valuation.

By late April, an estimated 45% of the public float was sold short (up from ~18% shortly after listing). This short interest continued to climb into late Q2. As of mid-June, reported short interest ranged from ~32% up to 45% of free float, with some speculative reports even claiming >60% (though such figures may double-count intra-day churn). Even as the stock doubled, short selling continued. As of mid-June, around 32% of available shares were still on loan to short sellers, an extreme figure compared to names like Tesla (3%).

But the real pain came from the borrow cost, the price short sellers pay to hold their positions. While borrowing costs for mega-cap stocks like Apple or Tesla are usually under 1%, CoreWeave’s borrow fees exploded past 150% annualized in June, peaking near 343% at one point. These fees were driven by sheer scarcity: at one point, just 25,000 shares were left to borrow market-wide. This made short positions not only risky but incredibly expensive to maintain.

The result? A classic short squeeze. As the stock rose and losses mounted, more than $1.6 billion in paper losses for short sellers by mid-June, many were forced to cover. This buying pressure pushed the stock even higher, creating a feedback loop that intensified the rally. According to S3 Partners, CoreWeave’s “squeeze risk” index hit 100 in early May, the highest possible reading, signaling a perfect storm for shorts.

However, with some of the short positions already flushed out and borrow rates easing slightly (to around 197%), the worst of the squeeze may be behind us. Available shares to borrow are creeping back up (~250,000 by mid-June), and the stock’s rise has started to slow. That said, the short base remains large, meaning any new positive catalyst, like another major deal or an earnings beat, could reignite a second wave of short covering.

Still, it’s important to remember that high short interest reflects real skepticism. Analysts like Felix Wang at Hedgeye have maintained their bearish outlooks, arguing CoreWeave has no credible path to profitability over the next few years and is burning capital at an unsustainable rate. He, and others, remain short despite the pain, suggesting that conviction among bears hasn’t disappeared.

If fundamentals remain strong and execution persists, shorts can remain underwater. However, should growth stall or insider selling increase after the lock-up expires, the short thesis gains renewed traction with it, and consequently the stock's downside pressure.

Growth Catalysts and Upside Drivers

CoreWeave’s bull case rests on its role as a leading “AI hyperscaler”, providing the specialized cloud infrastructure needed to train and deploy advanced AI models. The company’s fundamentals are undeniably impressive in growth terms. In Q1 2025 (its first report as a public company), CoreWeave delivered $982 million in revenue, up 420% year-on-year. Management’s full-year 2025 guidance calls for ~$5 billion revenue (nearly 6x 2024 levels). Perhaps more striking is the backlog of contracted business: as of Q1, CoreWeave had $25.9 billion in revenue backlog, multi-year commitments from customers that provide clear visibility into future growth.

This backlog, which the company notes consists of long-term agreements with “success-based” capex matching (i.e. CoreWeave builds capacity as customers ramp up usage), gives credence to the idea that current demand is not just speculative, but contractually locked. For instance, the crown jewel is a five-year, $11.9 billion contract with OpenAI to supply dedicated cloud compute for OpenAI’s model training and inference needs. In addition, just last quarter CoreWeave signed an additional agreement worth up to $4 billion with OpenAI (extending through 2029) to further expand capacity. These deals alone solidify CoreWeave’s revenue base for years, assuming OpenAI continues to execute on its AI roadmap.

Another blockbuster catalyst emerged in Q2 2025: Google became a CoreWeave customer. In June, Reuters reported that CoreWeave has been tapped as a provider of GPU compute capacity to Google Cloud, which in turn will resell that capacity to OpenAI under Google’s new partnership with OpenAI. In effect, CoreWeave is helping Google meet OpenAI’s voracious AI compute demand (augmenting Google’s own data centers), a win-win that diversifies CoreWeave’s revenue beyond Microsoft. (Microsoft was CoreWeave’s largest early customer, accounting for ~62% of 2024 revenue, but Microsoft’s needs may evolve as it rethinks its data center strategy with OpenAI.)

Landing Google as a customer not only reduces over-reliance on any single channel, it also lends credibility and could open the door to other hyperscaler partnerships. Industry insiders note that having a deep-pocketed ally like Google improves CoreWeave’s ability to secure financing for its aggressive expansion plans.

Beyond these mega-deals, CoreWeave is rapidly expanding capacity and rolling out cutting-edge hardware, another upside catalyst for growth. In April 2025, CoreWeave became the first cloud provider to launch Nvidia’s new “Grace Blackwell” superchips at scale. These GPUs (part of Nvidia’s next-gen Blackwell architecture) deliver significant performance gains for AI workloads. CoreWeave’s early deployment enabled marquee clients like Cohere, IBM, and Mistral AI to start using Blackwell systems immediately.

The company even set an industry record in the MLPerf AI benchmark tests with a massive 2,500-GPU Blackwell cluster, demonstrating its technological edge. CoreWeave’s Blackwell-based cloud instances can scale to an astonishing 110,000 GPUs connected by Nvidia Quantum-2 networking, positioning it as a go-to platform for any AI lab needing extreme compute scale. This aggressive capacity build-out (with strong support from partner collaboration with Nvidia) will enable CoreWeave to grab new demand as next-generation AI models spread. For instance, should a wave of new AI startups or corporate AI efforts require cloud GPU muscle in 2025–2026, CoreWeave is better placed than even some of its giant cloud rivals to get them online fast with best-in-class hardware.

Finally, CoreWeave’s extensive partnerships create a virtuous loop: Nvidia’s support provides priority access to new chips; OpenAI’s pledges confirm the model and fuel scale; Google’s partnership diversifies and boosts CoreWeave’s reputation among cloud buyers. Even companies like Core Scientific (a former crypto miner pivoting to AI) have signed 12-year deals with CoreWeave to build out data centers to support its growth (e.g. a $4.7 billion investment for facilities in Texas).

Bottom Line

CoreWeave’s explosive rally has been driven by a rare convergence of structural demand, tight float dynamics, and AI infrastructure tailwinds. While the stock now reflects elevated expectations, its $25.9 billion revenue backlog, deep ties with OpenAI and Google, and first-mover deployment of Nvidia’s Blackwell chips position it as a foundational layer of the AI economy.

The upcoming lock-up expiration may introduce short-term volatility, but for investors with a 1–2 year horizon, any pullback could present an entry point into a company scaling faster and deeper, than most competitors. Execution, not sentiment, will determine whether CoreWeave’s valuation grows into a new baseline.

data.title: Zevia: Higher Valuation Provides A Weak Risk-To-Reward (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Zevia PBC (NYSE:ZVIA) has obstacles in the growth story. The sugar-free soda and energy drink company is eyeing increased marketing and distribution, which Zevia very clearly needs. Yet, the competitive landscape remains challenging, questioning Zevia’s growth story’s success.

I initiated the stock at a Hold rating in my initial March article, titled “Zevia: Distribution Gains Are Needed For Profitability”. The stock has since surged with a 55% return, significantly outpacing S&P 500’s return of 7%.

There Are Major Concerns in Zevia’s Growth Story

Zevia’s growth story comes with concerns. Notably, the first quarter revenues of $38.0 million declined by -2.0% year-on-year, exceeding Wall Street’s consensus by $0.7 million but still showing weak distribution gains as Zevia is trying to grow. Whereas the purchase frequency and dollar sales per trip KPIs improved, boosting growth, Zevia doesn’t seem to have gained growing consumer interest. The household penetration declined by 40 basis points to just 5.0% for the quarter. The weaker household penetration is concerning; Zevia needs to capture good consumer interest for a good on-shelf performance.

Without better brand interest from consumers, retailers seem unlikely to allocate Zevia the increased shelf space that the company needs. Zevia’s previous loss in the club channel and one lost customer in the mass channel offset the company’s distribution expansion with Walmart (WMT), driving the concerning Q1 decline in household penetration. Zevia has secured some distribution wins with Walgreens and eyes expanded distribution with Albertsons (ACI), but the deals don’t seem to be enough to increase Zevia’s total distribution too significantly. The company still needs much more extensive distribution.

To improve brand recognition, Zevia has focused an increasing amount of resources on marketing, including more than doubled marketing spend in Q1 at a total of $6.2 million or 16.2% of revenues. The significant marketing campaign with Jelly Roll, launched in March, has driven a great number of impressions across media channels, according to Zevia. Yet, the competitive landscape remains challenging, making the branding effort’s success uncertain - soda shelves are already dominated by Coca-Cola (KO), PepsiCo (PEP), and Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP), and the current contest for energy drink shelf space is even more intense with Red Bull, Monster (MNST), Celsius (CELH), and others that are causing intense competitive pressure. The established competitors’ much larger scale allows much more marketing presence, and breaking into the industry with much less resources against well-known brands is likely to remain challenging for Zevia. Zevia’s increased marketing spend with the Jelly Roll campaign needs to produce very good results in upcoming quarters. Revenues are still guided at $158-163 million for 2025, suggesting slow 3.5% growth at the mid-point.

A second major concern, tied closely to the sales performance, is Zevia’s profitability. While the company’s productivity initiative has driven great reductions in selling expenses and general & administrative costs, and the gross margin has benefited from lower product costs and improved inventory management, Zevia still operates at a significant loss. Adjusted EBITDA came in at -$3.3 million for Q1, and the adjusted EBITDA loss is guided at -$8-11 million for 2025.

The continued losses again underline Zevia’s need for more distribution and sales – as I wrote in my initial article, the beverage industry comes with significant benefits from scale, and Zevia’s scale isn’t currently large enough to achieve good profitability in the industry’s competitive landscape. Despite a great amount of savings from the productivity initiative, Zevia won’t be able to save itself into profitability. As such, the focus point remains clear for the short- to mid-term – the company needs increased distribution and accelerated topline growth through great marketing efforts. The reaffirmed 2025 revenue guidance doesn’t suggest enough sales acceleration after a weak 2024 yet. A strong balance sheet does give Zevia some flexibility over the mid-term, with a $27.7 million net cash position to fuel growth investments.

Zevia’s Higher Valuation Provides a Weak Risk-to-Reward

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Zevia for a base scenario. I now assume quite similar revenue growth, accelerating within 2025 from some distribution gains and more significant marketing efforts. I now assume a 2024-2034 CAGR of 5.3% with 2.5% perpetual growth afterward in the base scenario DCF model.

I again assume the EBIT margin to slowly improve to a good 9% level. While the level clearly trails many beverage giants’ levels, Zevia’s much smaller scale doesn’t enable as good profitability. For now, the 9% EBIT margin forecast is also highly uncertain as profitability is based on many volatile factors. I still expect Zevia’s cash flow conversion to be good.

The estimates put Zevia’s fair value at $1.97, 38% below the stock price at the time of writing – the stock is valued unattractively after the stock’s surge. Although there could still be significant upside potential if Zevia’s growth story turns out to be a massive success, the current performance doesn’t signal such a scenario to be likely. The valuation sets Zevia’s risk-to-reward at a weak level.

The fair value estimate is down from $2.27 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital (WACC) of 10.27%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I still don’t expect Zevia to draw any debt. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.40% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.33% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.24. With a liquidity premium of 0.5%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 10.27%.

Takeaway

Zevia’s performance underlines remaining concerns in the growth story, as the beverage industry is challenging to break into with small scale. Profitability remains highly negative despite great cost savings, as Zevia clearly needs to grow its topline for more sustainable profitability. The higher stock price makes Zevia’s risk-to-reward unattractive, and as such, I downgrade my rating on the stock to sell.

data.title: Super Micro: Avoid FOMO, As Best Days Are Well Over (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Super Micro: Solid Recovery, What's Next?

A pretty good month for investors of Super Micro Computer, Inc. (NASDAQ:SMCI), as they attempted to squeeze out the intransigent SMCI short sellers, even as the leading AI rack-scale hardware company has mounted an encouraging comeback against all odds. The market seems to have moved on valiantly from the pessimism that nearly doomed the stock as it fell unceremoniously to its late 2024 lows. Hence, I believe bullish investors would likely regard the recent outperformance favorably at a critical time, as the AI infrastructure thesis has received an opportune revival.

Even as the market bolstered the stock of Super Micro's leading semiconductor customers (SMH) and big tech (XLK), SMCI is still languishing well below the zenith it scaled in early 2024. In other words, I assess that even as XLK surged to a new high and the SMH inched closer to its 2025 heights, SMCI's buying momentum has failed to follow through convincingly.

There haven't been shortages of uplifting reports from its key customers recently that should have improved SMCI's buying sentiments even further. NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) has gone on an illustrious whirlwind tour in the Middle East with President Trump, with Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) also in attendance. Joined by the regal entourage of the AI fraternity, Nvidia and its American peers struck noteworthy partnerships with key American ME partners.

In my previous SMCI write-up, I argued that the market is likely looking forward to an improved ramp cadence for Blackwell in 2025. Based on Nvidia's recent earnings update, it seems like the momentum is expected to follow through favorably.

Moreover, the burgeoning opportunities for Super Micro in the ME have also been lifted by its recent DataVolt partnership, highlighting the immense opportunities availing to America's AI value chain as the proliferation of the AI infra build-out starts ramping up outside the US. Yet, investors must also scrutinize the execution phase on the logistical, talent resource pool, supply chain, and critically, and how the White House intends to implement the bilateral chips export framework for the Middle East. These are nascent developments that aren't likely to be straightforward, suggesting that there could be unanticipated delays that should be accounted for these opportunities to be realized.

In other words, I believe that the recent surge in SMCI stock as it outperformed the S&P 500 (SPX) (SP500) admirably since my previous update may have been overstated. Despite that, SMCI's chip design customers have continued to underpin the bullish sentiments that also saw the SMH outperform its software peers (IGV) recently. AMD's recent AI day has bolstered Wall Street's confidence that AMD's rack scale solutions could provide Nvidia a run for its money from 2026, lifting the bullishness in AMD stock as well.

Nvidia's forays into Europe to help build out the EU's AI architecture and accelerate its game in the AI race is noteworthy. Yet, we must also scrutinize Europe's regulatory frameworks, and possible reticence in matching America's gumption to propel their efforts. The EU bureaucracy could also hinder a more expeditious approach to accelerate the region's AI ambitions, suggesting that unless Nvidia updates us with a step function change in revenue estimates from ME or its European customers, overstating our conviction might end up disappointing us.

And given SMCI's core reliance on the underlying demand dynamics of Nvidia and big tech to help drive another revenue reacceleration opportunity, Super Micro's prospects are intricately linked to its semi partners in the AI value chain.

Super Micro's Peak Growth May Hamper Bullish Sentiments

Notwithstanding these positive developments, I don't see it likely that Wall Street will lift Super Micro's revenue growth deceleration trajectory over the next two years. While the proliferation of AI infra beyond the US represents a tantalizing prospect to bolster SMCI's thesis, the execution requires finesse while also considering substantive regulatory and geopolitical constraints. Until these are resolutely ironed out, the focus should remain predicated on the CapEx growth thesis from America's leading hyperscaler customers and big tech proponents.

Yet, the growing concern of Super Micro's profitability volatility could have peaked, as Blackwell scales. In addition, SMCI is also well-positioned to benefit from AMD's improved tech stack as it seeks to penetrate further into Nvidia's dominance from 2026. Yet, I must also recognize that Super Micro's fast go-to-market cadence may matter less as the spurt in AI infra investments starts to taper from 2026.

Investors are urged to take heed of the CapEx guidance to be promulgated by big tech during their Q4 earnings conference, but that wouldn't be available until early next year. Hence, the trepidation of a further slowdown in AI CapEx and execution risks afflicting the ME and European AI bonanza.

Buy Or Sell SMCI Stock?

With SMCI's price action demonstrating a robust recovery from its 2024 lows, I'm confident that we have observed the worst of its fallout. The series of higher-lows structures affirm my analysis, even though its relatively high short interest ratio indicates that bearish momentum could still return later.

SMCI's forward EBITDA multiple has also recovered to the 12.4x level, well above its long-term average of 8.8x. Since I'm less convinced that Super Micro can muster another topline/earnings-driven surge that took the stock to its 2024 zenith, the $50 resistance zone could prove to be another hindrance for buyers to surmount.

Hence, I assess that the mean-reversion opportunity for SMCI has already played out nicely, suggesting gains are arguably limited, unless the stock's fundamentals get a much-needed upgrade. In view of that, the risk/reward is conceivably more balanced at the current levels, and investors should consider taking a more circumspect posture while waiting for a pullback to transpire.

Rating: Downgrade to Hold.

data.title: Balchem: A Healthy Long-Term Player
data.description:
In the fall of 2023, I called Balchem (NASDAQ:BCPC) a strong performer, which saw a soft 2023. The long-term organic growth story saw flattish results at the time, but growth returned and margins have expanded, which has triggered a re-rating in the shares ever since.

By now earnings multiples have risen to the low thirties, which is supported by the long-term quality of the business, yet a fair valuation is something entirely different from an opportune time for investors, which I do not see at this point in time.

A Healthy Business

Founded back in 1967, Balchem has a mission to make the world a healthier place by providing trusted, innovative, and science-based solutions for health and nutritional needs in the world. The business went public soon thereafter and has thrived on the back of dominant market positions within niche segments of these larger markets.

The company generates nearly a billion in sales from various products and solutions that cater to these end markets. With a workforce of 1,400, the company is highly productive as this has been demonstrated in margins, with EBITDA reported at a quarter of a billion.

Revenues are split across three segments, the largest of which is the human nutrition and health segment, which in fact makes up nearly two-thirds of sales. This includes the production and sale of minerals, nutrients, vitamins, and micro-encapsulation technologies for various food markets.

The animal nutrition and health segment makes up nearly a quarter of ales with similar products and services offered in the human segment, but then, of course, for animals. Specialty products make up nearly 15% of sales with the segment providing critical sterilization technologies, with these products used in the medical device and food industries.

Over the past decade, the company has nearly doubled sales from just over half a billion to nearly a billion, resulting in solid growth. This has been achieved amidst a stable share count and rather stable margins.

These advancements have been reflected in the share price, which rose from $50 in 2015 to a high of $186 per share last year, with shares now trading at $160 per share. Shares have risen some 40% from the fall of 2023 when I believed that a high-twenties times earnings multiple was perhaps justifiable given the strong track record of the business, as I was looking for a better entry point.

Delivering, But Expensive

As it turned out, 2023 has been a tougher year in which revenues fell by 2% to $922 million, while adjusted earnings per share were down three cents to $4.00 per share.

Growth returned in 2024, with revenues up just over 3% to $953 million, although this understates the extent of the improvements, with EBITDA up over 8% to $250 million, and adjusted earnings up by similar percentages to $4.37 per share, with an earnings number which looks quite clean. Net debt was down to $140 million, for a leverage ratio well below 1 times. This allowed the company to hike the dividend by more than 10%, being the 16 consecutive hikes, with dividends payable at $0.87 per share each year.

In April, the company reported a solid start to 2025 with first-quarter sales seen up more than 4%. Amidst real margin gains, adjusted earnings rose by nineteen cents to $1.22 per share. Net debt is pretty stable at $140 million as nearly 33 million shares value equity of the business at nearly $5.3 billion, for a roughly $5.4 billion enterprise valuation. While leverage remains minimal at just over a quarter of a billion, leverage ratios barely surpass 0.5 times, yet earnings multiples are demanding. Pegging realistic earnings around $4.75–$5.00 per share at this point, this results in an elevated 32–34 times earnings multiple.

This is not too uncommon for the business, which delivers, now, reporting on roughly 10% earnings per share growth driven by a combination of solid organic growth and margin gains. This acceleration of growth, after a few tougher years, has re-rated the shares again, but this time in a higher interest rate environment.

All of this makes me somewhat cautious here, although there is nothing wrong with the quality of the business, just the market's appraisal of the business.

What Now?

The truth is that Balchem remains a very quality business and commands premium multiples, like it has done in the past, and still managed to deliver great returns for investors. However, the company has seen higher multiples with the passage of time, while frankly, underlying growth has been somewhat slower in recent years.

This set-up makes me cautious after the company has seen solid returns from my take in 2023. Amidst all this, I am not willing to be involved here, but amidst deleveraging efforts, there is more financial room for M&A efforts.

In anticipation of that, I look forward to updating my constructive view on the business and long-term investment thesis, but for now, do not see a reason to get involved.

data.title: Is ViX Streaming The Catalyst For The Grupo Televisa Shares?
data.description:
The Grupo Televisa Value Disappearance

Grupo Televisa (TV) is suffering from low share prices and a suppressed valuation.

From the above share price graph over the last 30 years, the straightforward conclusion is that shares have lost nearly all their value. The share price at the point of the graph that is highlighted by the orange box with the date "Jun 15 2015", was just over $39. The current trading price, 10 years later almost to the day, is only ~$2.25.

Note that the declining share price represents a loss of value in the company, amounting to over 90% over the last decade. Dilution through new equity issuance or growing debt don't explain even a small part of the decline that the graph is showing. It's virtually all a loss of company value. The market has simply lost confidence when it comes to Grupo Televisa.

Previous Seeking Alpha articles, such as this one, have gone into the low company value extensively. Generally, reading those articles and other company reviews about Grupo Televisa, the sentiment is that the company is undervalued, but a growth catalyst to bring the value out is missing.

The Seeking Alpha factor grades, shown below, also tell this story. Every factor is on green/deep green, except for the growth factor that has the lowest score possible, a deep dark red "F":

The wait for a growth catalyst may soon be over, however. That is, if it has indeed been hidden away in the realm of streaming, where encouraging developments are taking place for Grupo Televisa.

Thesis: ViX Streaming As A Catalyst For Recovery

To immediately get right to the thesis at the core of this article: a catalyst seems to have appeared that could drive the share price of Grupo Televisa back up significantly. This catalyst is the rapid emergence of the Spanish media streaming service "ViX". Grupo Televisa has a large stake in this service through the ownership of 45% of the shares of TelevisaUnivision, the company that operates ViX.

As a side note, the link to ViX on the internet only works in certain countries, but this link to a description gives you a nice high-level summary of what the service is all about.

ViX is a relatively new streaming service, launched just over three years ago in February 2022, and it's sometimes dubbed "the Netflix of Latin America". This is quite a statement and there's definitely some remarks to be made with the comparison of ViX to the market leader in streaming, but it's true that ViX has become the leading Spanish media streaming service globally in the short timespan since the launch. The merger of Televisa and Univision in 2020-2022 paved the way for the new service to reach this status so soon. The two entities jointly have a footprint that provides near immediate access to the Hispanic people in both Mexico and the US, around one-third of all Spanish-speaking people globally.

ViX is now already bigger than the Atresplayer service by Spanish Atresmedia (OTCPK:AIOSF) (OTCPK:ATVDY). This is one comparable streaming service with its roots in Spain. It has content for global Spanish audiences as well, created with the support of subsidiary Buendia estudios, that Atresmedia jointly owns with Telefónica (TEF).

This article focuses on ViX, but Grupo Televisa also owns two telecoms access businesses, i.e. a cable business and the Sky satellite TV business. These are both suffering a slow decline. Revenue has been going down to single-digit every year over the last few years. ViX streaming is thus a most welcome contrast to the access part of the business, because it's a fast growth area. In fact, if the ViX streaming service indeed grows anywhere near how Netflix has grown, it can be the catalyst that drives Grupo Televisa stock up again.

Note that I use the word "if" in the last sentence. The next section aims to show that there's many reasons to be enthusiastic about ViX, but that Netflix-type growth seems a stretch.

ViX Streaming: The Product

So, first some good news about the ViX streaming service. It has at least three key building blocks to become successful: (1) it has an unsurpassed Spanish language media library, (2) it has two fitting choices in price-plans, ViX and ViX+, to cater to both people who prefer freemium Advertising Video-On-Demand ("AVOD") and people who prefer to pay a Subscription to enjoy the Video-On-Demand free from ads ("SVOD"), and (3) it has flexible distribution. TelevisaUnivision enables integration of the ViX media library in other streaming services.

ViX Streaming: The Addressable Market

Looking at the addressable market for the ViX service, there's Spanish-speaking audiences in Mexico and the US at the core. One notable example of how ViX reaches these audiences is the recent deal with Charter Communications (CHTR) to make the streaming service available for Charter's Spectrum TV customers in the US. There's more deals like this at play. As ~20% of the US population speaks Spanish, roughly ~65M people, the Charter deal strengthens ViX's reach significantly.

As an example of Mexico, ViX obviously reached people over Grupo Televisa's own TV services in Mexico. Grupo Televisa also has an access services footprint outside Mexico, so this audience is also easily reached.

Next to the US and Mexican markets, it wouldn't take too much effort to target the remaining part of the 600M Spanish-speaking people globally. That's the big advantage of having a scalable online streaming platform. As one example of an initiative outside the US and Mexico, ViX recently partnered with Atresmedia, to enter Spain by integrating the ViX media library in the Atresplayer streaming service. Similarly, ViX will seek to enter other countries where there's Spanish-speaking audiences.

The current status is that ViX recently reached the 50M monthly average uniques ("MAU") milestone. This heavy traffic comes from the over 7M paying subscribers and a much larger group of users of the free ad-supported ViX service. TelevisaUnivision doesn't provide any consistent detailed reporting about the ViX user base, but milestones are occasionally shared in the press.

Now that we have the above ballpark figures for the current ViX user base, we still need an estimate for the achievable size of the user base in the end state to see what a growth path could look like.

Looking at Netflix (NFLX), various open sources state that Netflix has ~300M paying subscribers and a fast-growing freemium/ad-supported tier, already reaching nearly 100M users in addition. There's 1.5B English-speaking people globally. So with the total of 400M Netflix users, this means that the service reaches over 25% of the addressable market of English speakers. This excludes additional viewers in Netflix households, of course, but it's a ballpark figure. It also leaves out that Netflix reaches non-English speakers, but let's assume that this is, relatively, unimportant.

If ViX could, similar to Netflix, also reach 25% of the Spanish speakers globally, the user base would be 150M (=25% of 600M) in the saturated situation. This would, similar to the Netflix example, then also exclude additional people in Spanish-speaking households. Taking the current 50M monthly uniques that reportedly use ViX, this means ViX can triple in size from where it's today.

ViX Streaming: The Growth Path

Starting from the current number of ~50M MAU, TelevisaUnivsion expects further growth in the upcoming quarters and years, but there's only snippets of information available when it comes to the outlook. With no full disclosure of current user and subscriber numbers, the longer-term outlook is even more of a guess. It's good news that the user base already reached the 50M mark, but what is the growth path to the achievable ViX user base over the next years?

It has taken Netflix over a quarter of a decade to build their user base. Note that growth of any new service, according to the S-curve growth model or the hockey stick model, is generally fastest after the first few start-up years and before the last tougher years when the market is getting close to saturation. With Netflix, the growth rate had a slow start in the first three years of existence until it exploded with a growth of over 600% in the year 2000, four years after Netflix was founded. Then it grew fast for many years.

What does this mean in terms of ViX growth rate? Assuming that it will take 20 more years to achieve the maximum size in terms of users, the 150M mark, a ViX CAGR ("Compound Annual Growth Rate") of "only" ~6% would be needed. Alternatively, taking double-digit growth as an assumption, it would take between 11 and 12 years for ViX to reach 150M monthly uniques,. This is if ViX grows the base with a 10% CAGR annually from now onward.

In my opinion, it's a fair expectation, and frankly not overly ambitious, that TelevisaUnivision grows the ViX service double-digit, still only in the fourth year of existence. Available numbers from press articles, with the 40M MAU milestone reported in October 2023 and the 50M three quarters later, suggest a growth rate of the monthly unique visitors of well over 25% on a yearly basis. This is the growth of MAUs, which is different from the growth of paying subscribers. The growth rate of the paying subscribers is likely well lower than 25%. I'd assume that the growth of ViX monthly unique visitors is heavily skewed towards the AVOD users getting to know the service.

There's many remarks with the above growth outlook for ViX. Some important ones are:

  • The Netflix growth numbers from early years were related to paying subscribers (SVOD-numbers). Today, AVOD users, those opting to use the service for free with advertising, are much more important. An AVOD user is much less valuable than an SVOD user, though.
  • Netflix had a first mover advantage when growing to its massive size that it has today. ViX is a late entrant in comparison, facing ample competition in the streaming market.
  • Spanish-speaking people are often already using streaming services offered by US companies, even with English content. For example, Netflix and HBO Max are much bigger in Mexico than ViX. Again, there's thus heavy competition for audience/eyeballs, posing a challenge for ViX to maintain high-growth rates over years to come.
  • Netflix had a period of extreme growth from the "growth inflection point" in the fourth year of existence when it grew over 600%. This marked the start of Netflix's steepest growth in the S-curve. It's not realistic by a long stretch that ViX can achieve such triple-digit hypergrowth numbers for one year, let alone for a few years in a row.
  • Finally, it doesn't seem fair to assume that a service for Spanish audiences has the same monetization potential as a similar service for English audiences. Advertising and subscription fees will yield significantly less in Spanish-speaking markets. For example, Latin-American households spend roughly half (<$500 annually) of the amount that American households spend on communications services (>$1,000 annually).

To conclude, ViX streaming has lots of room to grow. Looking at the addressable market, it should be possible to grow the user base three times. Times are very different now, then when Netflix entered the market, though. Low double-digit growth rates for ViX SVOD users and growth of monthly uniques of 20- 30% seems feasible, but triple-digit growth rates like with Netflix in the early years are not realistic.

What Does ViX Bring Grupo Televisa Investors?

To summarize the benefit logic in a few sentences: Grupo Televisa owns 45% of TelevisaUnivision. Grupo Televisa is, through that ownership, also the major owner of ViX. An increasing value of the ViX streaming platform should benefit the Grupo Televisa shares.

To put the above into perspective again: Netflix currently has a market cap of ~$521B and a modest ~$15B in debt. The market cap of Grupo Televisa is between $1 and $1.5B currently, insignificant in comparison with the streaming market leader.

If the growth trajectory for ViX, as described in the section above, would continue, Grupo Televisa's current market cap could seem a ridiculously low number a few years from now. To recap, ViX can expect a user base of ~150M users in the saturated situation. This is smaller than Netflix with 400M users, but it's still three times as high as today's ~50M. ViX has a significantly lower monetization potential than Netflix due to the different Spanish and English demographics. Even taking into account these constraints, ViX would be a very valuable asset in comparison with Grupo Televisa's market cap today.

Let's say that ViX would "only" reach a value of 1/20th of the market cap of Netflix, to take a low-end estimate, it would already be worth $26B (=521/20). This lower-end estimate for the ViX value would take into account all kinds of adversities and margins of safety:

  • A smaller Spanish ViX total user base of 150M vs the 400M Netflix
  • The ViX user base skewed towards AVOD users, while Netflix is skewed to SVOD
  • A low-end growth trajectory, low double-digit in the fourth year of existence for ViX as opposed to triple-digit in the fourth year of existence for Netflix
  • Lower monetization potential in Spanish markets in comparison with English markets
  • Intensifying competition for ViX due to a response from the other streaming services existing in the Spanish markets. Netflix, for example, recently announced the plan to invest heavily in Mexico.

When ViX reaches a low-end estimated value of $26B, Grupo Televisa would own 45% of that, equating to $11.7B. This is tenfold the current market cap of Grupo Televisa.

The above is, of course, a very rough, back-of-an-envelope calculation of what the ViX streaming service can bring to Grupo Televisa. The aim is to take a high-end estimate to show that ViX streaming is likely to serve as a growth catalyst in years to come.

There's many remarks that can be made with the above estimate of the value of ViX a few years from now. There's also lots of work that can be done on scenarios. This is perhaps for another time, but I'd like to mention here that the above ignores debt. TelevisaUnivision currently has between $9 and $10B in debt on its balance sheet. Grupo Televisa "owns" 45% of that debt, so it's a very significant amount in comparison with the value of the ViX streaming platform at the moment. In addition, Grupo Televisa also has debt itself. This is a modest leverage of ~2.9 net debt/EBITDA, while TelevisaUnivision is heavily indebted with a leverage that's twice as high, ~5.8 net debt/EBITDA.

Risks To The Thesis

The previous section already mentioned many risks when determining a low-end value for ViX. This was only limited to the ViX streaming platform, however. There's other risks for Grupo Televisa shareholders as well. One that needs some attention is the corporate structure discount. This risk refers to a situation where shareholders of one company, in this case Grupo Televisa, own a certain valuable asset through ownership in another company, in this case TelevisaUnivision, but it doesn't get reflected in the share price. A higher share price only follows if the market acknowledges the value of the asset and drives the share price up. In the case of Grupo Televisa, even when ViX grows double-digit in the coming years, the shares may keep trading at depressed levels. Such a situation can persist for years. Investors should be aware in advance that there can be a long wait for a return when buying Grupo Televisa shares. Luckily, the current gross dividend yield on the Grupo Televisa shares of over 4% means that investors are at least getting some return while waiting for the shares to start reflecting the value of ViX streaming.

On top of the above-mentioned risk, it's not even guaranteed that the Grupo Televisa shares keep their current value. The shares, although very cheap at the moment, may still go lower. This can happen, for example, in case the slow decline of the cable and satellite businesses becomes worse.

Conclusion And Investor Takeaway

The ViX streaming service has the right building blocks to build out the leadership position in Spanish media streaming. There's a massive addressable market. ViX can potentially threefold the current user base, so there's good growth prospects. Even with modest assumptions, the ViX streaming platform is set to become a very valuable asset for Grupo Televisa. Over the next decade, the value of ViX may well grow to exceed the entire market cap of Grupo Televisa multifold.

There's plenty of uncertainties and risks for ViX streaming in the coming years, both for the growth of the user base and for the monetization potential. Next to that, Grupo Televisa shareholders also have to take into account the other risks, like a persisting corporate structure discount and the deterioration of the access business.

Investing in Grupo Televisa is not a guaranteed slam dunk, but the prospects for ViX make Grupo Televisa shares an investment with a fair chance of doubling or tripling over the coming years.

data.title: AMC Networks: Reiterating Strong Buy On Proactive Debt Management
data.description:
AMC Networks (AMCX) is a media and entertainment company that is currently trading at all-time lows. The company is facing a heavy debt-load and suffers from cord-cutting trends in their legacy business segment. Since our last article the company's share price stayed relatively stable from $5.83 at the time of writing the last article to $6.03 today.

In this article, we want to analyse recent developments at the company, including recently announced debt measures.

Clown in a Cornfield

In May, AMCX released "Clown in a Cornfield" to theatres in the US. With a reported budget of just $1m and a box office of $9.5m, this shows the financial attractiveness of low-budget niche content. While $9.5m in box office does not really move the needle for the company as a whole, it shows the potential returns for the kind of content many of AMCX's subsidiaries focus on.

Net of theatres' cuts and cuts, net revenue for the company could be as high as $4.5m, representing a 450% ROI for this movie, and this is before the movie hit streaming or physical releases.

Clown in a Cornfield has franchise potential, as the book already has two more sequels written. I want to draw some attention to a somewhat similar case, with a franchise called "Terrifier". Looking at the different Terrifier releases, the growth is astonishing. The first Terrifier installation earned just $340k in Box Office, but the second already rose to $15.6m. The third installation became a huge success and windfall for Cineverse (CNVS) earning over $76m in worldwide box office with a budget of just $2m.

Of course, the success of something like Terrifier was a huge surprise for everyone involved and is unlikely to be easily replicated. However, this example does show the upside potential for this kind of content. While the chances are low, the chance of an upside surprise for any of the content AMCX produces does make the company ever so slightly more attractive for investors.

Debt Deals

Recently, AMCX announced two debt deals aimed at managing their debt proactively. Based on some other information their recent press releases reveal, AMCX's strategy can be summarised in three steps:

1. Buy Back some 2029 Notes in the Open Market

In the company's Q1 2025 release, AMCX revealed, that in April 2025 they repurchased $32m principal amount of its 4.25% senior notes due February 2029 in the open market. With a reported discount of $9m, this means that they paid around $23m for these notes, which translates to a price of roughly 72 cents on the dollar.

We will go over the tender offer in detail in the paragraph below, however some of the information revealed in the press release shows some further open market purchases the company has made since the Q1 2025 release. Based on the tender offer, as of June 17th, 2025, the aggregate principal amount of 4.25 2029 notes outstanding was just $886m.

Based on the Q1 2025 release, we know that as of March 31st, there as $985m principal outstanding for the notes in question. In the same release it was revealed that in April AMCX has repurchased $32m in principle, which put the principal outstanding at $953m. If on June 17th the outstanding principal was just $886m, this means that between April and June 17th, the company had purchased another $67m on the open market.

With the price of the notes in question slightly rising in the timeframe between April and June, we estimate the average price of these open market purchases to have been around 75 cents on the dollar.

2. Tender Offer for $450m of the Notes

On June 17th, AMCX has announced a Tender Offer for up to $450m Principal of the 4.25% notes due 2029. Including a potential early tender premium, they offer a total consideration of 81.5 cents on the dollar for these notes, which is significantly higher than the price the notes were trading at during the open market purchases mentioned before. Based on the price, we expect the tender offer to be completely filled and even potentially extended for more principal. However, for our calculations going forward we will assume that they will leave it at $450m for now.

If the Tender Offer gets fully filled at the early tender price, this would allow the company to buy back $450m principal for a total of $367m.

3. Offer new 10.5% Notes due 2032

To finance these deals and repay other indebtedness, the company has announced new 10.5% Notes due 2032 for a total of $400m. While the 10.5% obviously is much higher than the 4.25% of interest for the other notes, the maturity is 3 years later.

Debt Deals Summarised

To summarise the debt deals, AMCX has first bought back around $99m principal for an estimated $74m in the open market. Then they made a tender offer for $450m principal for a maximum of $367m and offered $400m new 10.5% notes due 2032.

After the completion of these deals, the company will have paid a total of around $441m to repurchase $549m principal amount of 4.25% notes and offered $400m of new 10.5% notes due 2032.

The purchases at a discount will result in an immediate gain of around $108m for the company and save around $23m in yearly interest expense. The new notes will cost $42m in interest yearly. In total, the company will absorb $20m more in interest yearly for an immediate gain of $108m and 3 years later maturity for $400m of their debt.

In our view, this is a huge win for AMCX, as the immediate gain will pay for the next 5 years of extra interest the company has to pay for the 10.5%, and now the company has spread out their maturities significantly, greatly reducing the risk of default in 2029.

Valuation

Revisiting our valuation metrics from our last articles, using a TTM Adjusted Operating Income of $517m and adjusting the EV to around $100m for the reduction in debt slightly offset by the small increase in stock price to an EV of $1.64m, AMCX is currently trading at an estimated EV/AOI of less than 3.2.

As peer Seeking Alpha's comparison, the bigger peers PARA and WBD are trading at an EV/EBITDA of above 8. We continue to believe that at current prices AMCX is extremely undervalued. At a market cap of just $280m the stock trades almost like an option on whether or not AMCX will default on their debt until 2029.

Based on the extreme cost discipline and the proactive debt management, we are increasingly optimistic about AMCX's ability to successfully execute on their turnaround. As the conversion price for the convertible notes is $12.74 our price target continues to be lower than the conversion price but still at a higher EV/EBITDA multiple than now, resulting in a reiteration of our price target at $12 per share.

data.title: Marvell: We Were Wrong About The Short Term (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Marvell (NASDAQ:MRVL) held their Custom AI Investor Event after the bell yesterday, and the stock is on a high because of it, trading as much as 10% since market open and up 7% to $74.95 per share. The big show boosted Wall Street’s confidence in Marvell’s addressable market for data centers at $94B by 2028, which is roughly 26% more than what management shared at last year’s event and indicates a 35% CAGR from 2023-2028 versus the previous estimate for 28%. Marvell’s fast track to get in on that market is its custom accelerated compute, which alone is expected to hit $55.4B by 2028, including $40.8B from custom XPU and $14.6B from XPU attach.

FYI: XPUs are basically a custom AI accelerators that provide the processing power to support AI infrastructure. We can’t prove or disprove the company’s 2028 TAM, and management definitely seems to know more about two years from now than it does about the near-term. Having said that, from the current visibility we have on Marvell, the stock is prone to a bounce back after the 34% dip YTD, and the market’s reaction to the Custom AI Investor event proves investor appetite for so-called beat-up AI growth stories is high.

We were wrong on the short term

We reiterated sell on Marvell earlier this month after rival Broadcom’s (AVGO) earnings results caused the latter to trade down, and that was a wrong call. This was not because the fundamental analysis was wrong, but because we didn’t listen to the stock price performance YTD, which, at the time, was down roughly 42%. Our bearish thesis was based on our belief that Marvell has a higher risk profile because of two things. 1. Street expectations for 40% growth in AI-related revenues would be harder to achieve, and 2. the AI custom ASIC tailwind was already priced into the stock, and the negatives of the resulting margin contraction due to that business was not. We continue to believe this thesis is at play in the mid-to-long run, but what we failed to realize is that the near-term negatives of this bearish thesis were already priced into the stock, particularly with the stock selling off post-earnings two quarters in a row. We think Marvell makes for an attractive trade after the sell-off YTD, and management has a shiny enough target, shown below, to continue supporting investor confidence in the near term.

Valuation of a beaten-down AI stock

Marvell isn’t cheap, but it’s not as expensive as it used to be. The stock does trade at premium multiples. Marvell now trades at Price to Sales of 9x versus a sector median of 3x, and its forward EV/Sales is 7.76x versus a group average of 2.87x. The chart below showcases Marvell's valuation.

While Marvell does look expensive, once it’s contextualized, it looks much more attractive. The chart below pegs Marvell’s EV/Sales against Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), Nvidia (NVDA), and Broadcom, and Marvell is surfing the bottom, along with AMD, which is our other trade pick for the next two quarters based on the negatives being priced in and the server CPU roadmap. The idea here is that both Nvidia and Broadcom have healthier fundamentals and a more favorable risk-reward in the mid to long term for their position within the supply chain. AMD and Marvell, on the other hand, are attractive because they’ve sold out so much YTD, so the worst is out of the way.

What happened at the event?

Street optimism is building after this week’s event, specifically regarding the market opportunity for the custom AI ASIC business line, or what Marvell calls XPUs. Barclays is eyeing “a $75 billion revenue pipeline consisting of >50 sockets across over 10 customers,” but disclaims that “these potential wins won’t contribute to revenue until 2028 at the earliest.” Morgan Stanley also sees custom being the fuel to the Marvell growth story, noting the company’s targeting of 20% of the market, with over half of the data center revenue coming from its custom business. Bank of America even raised its price target on Marvell to $90 per share for a 20% upside, while Lipacis reiterated a $133 price target. These positives won’t show up until 2028. Still, the existence of this number cannot truly be proved or disproved at the current moment, so this target against what’s been a rough year so far for Marvell provides investors with something to look forward to and make any short-term struggles less painful, especially as the company works for custom to make up from >25% custom in 4QFY25 to >50% in the future.

The event wasn’t all talk about TAM; there were some new hot updates on the table. Marvell introduced two new XPU sockets as well as four new XPU attach sockets, which are being worked on for hyperscaler customers. The company revealed the industry’s first 2nm custom Static RAM for next-gen AI infrastructure silicon, which is the highest bandwidth per sq mm with up to six gigabits of high-speed memory. Custom SRAM also saves up to 15% of the total area of a 2nm design, which allows for better utilization of recovered silicon real estate. With this product, Marvell is taking a step forward in its custom silicon tech, and it is addressing the memory problem when it comes to AI, which Alan Weckel captured, saying:

Memory remains one of the biggest challenges for AI clusters and clouds. These systems need as much memory as they can get as fast as they can...

The company also brought its total custom silicon wins to 18, as shown below.

The company’s largest customer remains Amazon (AMZN), which has been supporting its growth story, with the latter’s Trainium series being the biggest driver. AWS CEO Matt Garman totted up Marvell’s horn, noting the following about the collaboration:

Building a cost and power efficient cloud at the scale that only AWS can deliver begins with leading-edge semiconductors designed to meet the demanding infrastructure needs of our customers. Our expanded collaboration with Marvell enables us to deploy our comprehensive semiconductor portfolio and specialized networking hardware to advance our mission to provide the industry’s most robust and scalable cloud and AI services to our customers.

Marvell is expanding its customer base, and while we do believe Broadcom has the same customers and added ones, the current trajectory of CapEx spend makes us believe that there is enough to go around for the near-term. In the mid-term, we think Broadcom will be more attractive than Marvell as it has the advantage of scale and cost structure, plus a networking business to help offset the margin contraction caused by custom ASIC. With this in mind, Marvell is more prone to a short-term bounce back, even after the stock traded up Wednesday because it remains in the negative YTD and 6M charts while Broadcom is up 8% YTD and 12% 6M.

The TAM targets also make room for roadmap expansion, which is what investors will be watching for and which will raise the company's odds within the custom AI chip. We see more upside for Marvell as it puts together its custom silicon tech and regains its lost glory as an AI trade.

data.title: Ashtead: Lots Still To Look Forward To
data.description:
Ashtead (ASHTF) (OTCPK:ASHTY) (better known in the US by the name of its main business, Sunbelt Rentals, to which Ashtead plans to change its corporate name)

My last piece on Ashtead was my February “buy” piece, Ashtead: Long-Term Drivers Remain Positive, since when the share has moved up less than 1% according to SA, although the sterling-denominated London share has declined about 7% during that period. I saw that as a buying opportunity and added Ashtead to my portfolio for the first time.

At that time, I had concerns about what a weak economy and any construction downturn in its key U.S. market could mean for the company, but was upbeat about its proven success model and ongoing strategic focus.

Performance Last Year Was Solid, But Far From Stellar

This week the company published its full-year results, alongside unaudited results for the fourth quarter.

On balance, it was a fairly solid but unremarkable year. What the company calls its “rental revenue” rose 4%. Rather unhelpfully, this is constant currency revenue: the actual, reported revenue was down 1%. Operating profit fell 4%, while pre-tax profit and earnings per share both fell 5%.

The company’s cash flow generation potential was once more on show, with free cash flow generation of $1.8bn.

Local non-residential construction projects slowed in terms of there being more completions than starts and I see a risk that that trend will continue or even accelerate in the current economy, but megaproject activity e.g. data center construction remained robust and is set to grow significantly in the next couple of years, something that ought to benefit Ashtead given its strong U.S. presence through Sunbelt Rentals and high level of capex. Its capital investment last year was $2.4bn.

Ashtead Continues To Look Set For Growth

Given that industry context and Ashtead’s expenditure on being ready for growth, I am optimistic that we will see such growth in the next couple of years.

The company is currently implementing its Sunbelt 4.0 growth strategy and so far, this sounds as if it’s going well. In the first year of this plan, 61 locations were added to the company’s network, cross-selling effectiveness expanded and 42,000 new customers were added.

What we don’t know, however, is what this might look like in specifics of business performance over the next several years and beyond. After all, revenue slipped last year, notably in the North America General Tool division, and the same was too for profit. The main driver for that was a lower level of used equipment sales. But in an uncertain economic environment, how much can we (or not) expect Ashtead to grow in coming years?

The guidance for rental revenue growth this year (at constant currency) is 0-4%, while free cash flow (for some reason pinned at a fixed exchange rate of (in the case of sterling) $1.26 to the pound, some distance from today’s rate of $1.35) is given at $2.0-2.3bn. That is an attractive number, 10%-28% above last year’s already strong free cash flow performance. If it can achieve it – and Ashtead has typically delivered well in the past – then it helps the investment case and valuation, in my view.

Risks

Ashtead's heavy reliance on the North American market, specifically the U.S.A., makes it highly susceptible to any sustained downturn in construction activity there.

Last year's used tools sales revenues in the U.S. were weak, and I also see a risk that that trend could continue, again hurting this revenue stream for the company.

Valuation Still Looks Attractive

The company confirmed that it is on track to shift its primary listing to the U.S. in the first quarter of next year. The additional visibility and liquidity that offers could itself justify a rating upgrade given that the US market is generally higher valued than its London equivalent right now.

That said, United Rentals (URI) trades on a P/E ratio of 18 in the New York market, right in line with Ashtead’s current P/E ratio in London. So it remains to be seen whether the New York move will indeed help unlock a higher share price for Ashtead in the absence of other valuation rationales.

In my last piece, I saw Ashtead as offering long-term value. While the latest set of results were nothing to write home about (though perfectly acceptable) and the medium-term economic outlook is uncertain, over the long run I continue to think that Ashtead’s proven strengths as a business merit a premium valuation.

With its market cap of $26.3bn and net debt of $10.3bn, Ashtead currently commands an enterprise value of $36.6bn. That is around 17 times operating cash flow, compared to 15 at United Rentals. Again, then, the valuation is broadly in line with and in fact slightly higher than for United Rentals.

On those metrics, then, this does not look like a bargain, though if free cash flows are markedly higher this year as guided, I see the valuation as notably more attractive. However, I continue to see Ashtead as undervalued from a long-term perspective because it has already proven its business model, strategic power and ability to execute. The U.K. now represents just 8% of revenues and, with its far worse profit margins (last year, 7.6% compared to 32.5% and 32.7% for the two North American divisions), the ever-growing focus on its North American markets can only help Ashtead.

Given huge long-term demand (albeit construction is cyclical, a major watchout), Ashtead’s growth strategy and its expenditure to support that growth, I am optimistic that the company can continue its impressive recent growth trajectory, captured over the past five years in the image below.

On that basis, I continue to see it as offering attractive value for the long-term investor and accordingly maintain my “buy” rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: American States Water's Quality Is Intact, Its Valuation Isn't
data.description:
Thesis

My research argues that American States Water (NYSE:AWR) once sat on a throne of century-old prudence and relentless dividend hikes, but inflation, climate costs, clogged supply lines and a crankier regulator have ripped away the safety net, forcing management to thread every capital dollar through a needle even as the stock’s price tag still pretends predictability is a given.

American States Water Company Under Pressure

American States Water has long been the classic “set-and-forget” holding, its 69-year streak of dividend hikes embodying dependability. Yet stubborn wage inflation, tangled supply chains, elevated borrowing costs and a surge of pricey environmental mandates now collide with regulators who hesitate to approve full cost pass-throughs, forcing even this stalwart utility to rethink how it spends and how quickly it can earn those dollars back.

The squeeze is already showing up in the credit sheets.

Last year, a handful of utilities took downgrades after regulators cut back or delayed rate hikes. Then came Clyde, Texas, where a water-system failure turned deferred maintenance into a full-blown liquidity mess in just one quarter. Because the Environmental Protection Agency pegs required water and wastewater investment at roughly $1.2 trillion over the next 20 years, utilities that fall behind risk becoming price takers just when contractors are charging a premium for scarce crews and materials. AWR, which operates amid California’s strict oversight, therefore finds its trademark regulatory finesse necessary but no longer sufficient; it has to prioritize projects with almost surgical precision, lest capital dollars get trapped in queues while interest accrues on undepreciated assets.

Stack climate on top and things get worse. Longer fire seasons now chew up vegetation around Bear Valley’s electric lines, hotter summers increase system load, and rising insurance premiums quietly eat margin. Looking farther out, the looming post-2026 reduction in Colorado River allotments could tighten supply for the Southwest systems Golden State Water relies on, nudging treatment costs higher just when every penny matters. Federal disaster relief, once a reliable backstop, may also become a slower or smaller trickle as the Federal Emergency Management Agency juggles competing claims; consequently, utilities have to build thicker balance-sheet buffers of their own.

Yet, almost paradoxically, Washington’s policy pendulum is swinging in a way that re-energizes the electric side of the ledger. Because the Trump administration’s permitting reforms shorten approval timelines and the push for energy resilience enjoys bipartisan applause, grid expansion projects are moving from slide decks to shovels. After roughly 2 generations of demand stagnation, U.S. electricity consumption is now forecast to accelerate faster than at any point in the past 50 years, driven largely by data centers that alone could need an incremental 50 gigawatts by 2030. You may have noticed: in the first quarter of 2025 the S&P Utility Index rose 4.9% and the median regulated utility returned 9%, signaling that markets are willing to fund growth so long as earnings visibility improves. Even AWR’s relatively small Bear Valley Electric Service, which earned $0.07 a share this past quarter versus $0.05 a year ago, stands to benefit from the density of demand in California’s high-tech corridors.

Management’s ability to harvest that policy tailwind showed up early in 2025. Earnings per share climbed to $0.70, beating consensus by $0.03, while revenue grew 9.44% to $148.01 million. Those numbers sprang directly from the California Public Utilities Commission’s long-awaited approval of new rate cases: Golden State Water booked $0.52 a share, up from $0.48, and Bear Valley Electric pocketed the previously mentioned $0.07. Capital deployment is ramping in tandem. For calendar 2025, AWR intends to invest between $170 million and $210 million into regulated assets, and the CPUC has green-lit Golden State Water to lay out $573.1 million over the 2025–2027 span with mechanisms in place for prompt revenue recovery once projects close. Bear Valley has authority to spend $75.6 million, of which $23.1 million is fresh work expected to add roughly $3 million of annual revenue when rolled into rates, so the company can grow its electric rate base without stretching leverage.

As a matter of fact, the base is already expanding briskly: Golden State Water’s adopted average rate base went from $752.2 million in 2018 to $1.36 billion in 2024, a compound annual growth rate of 10.3%. If management executes its current pipeline on schedule, that upward trajectory should persist through the end of the cycle, cushioning earnings against the sector-wide cost creep that shows no sign of abating. Meanwhile, the contracted-services arm, American States Utility Services, did its part, contributing $0.13 a share as it assumed operations at Naval Air Station Patuxent River and Joint Base Cape Cod and secured $56.5 million in new military upgrade awards. Because construction activity was only modestly slowed by weather, ASUS entered Q2 with a record backlog, providing yet another lane of earnings visibility.

Crucially, AWR can pursue these investments without compromising its fortress balance sheet. Operating cash flow in the first quarter held steady at $45.1 million; a $50 million private note placement was completed, the revolving credit facility expanded to $195 million and pushed out to 2029, and the company still flaunts high-grade ratings of A from Standard & Poor’s, A+ for Golden State Water and A2 at Moody’s. Shareholders also keep seeing their checks grow: the dividend advanced from $0.45 in 2005 to $1.79 in 2024, rising at an 8.8% compound pace over the past 5 years and comfortably above management’s 7% target.

For anyone who invested $10,000 back in 2004 and reinvested distributions, the stake is now worth more than $74,000, a 10.3% annualized lift that even beats the S&P 500’s 8.12% over the same stretch. To fund growth without over-dialing debt, the at-the-market equity program has raised $117 million toward a $200 million goal; paced issuance means dilution stays manageable while fresh capital arrives just in time for shovel-ready projects.

American States Water Company: Challenges, Growth, and Valuation

Yet even as management leans into those policy-driven growth avenues, the very act of accelerating capital spend brings a fresh reckoning with valuation. Because every new dollar deployed eventually has to translate into durable, per-share returns, the next logical question is whether the market is already paying for that upside, or, perhaps, paying too much.

For anyone who has long treated American States Water Company as a sleepy, bond-like holding, the latest batch of figures lands with the uneasy realization that the company’s once-placid fundamentals and its still-premium valuation appear to be drifting apart.

Management concedes that the extra shares sold through its at-the-market program reduced first-quarter earnings by $0.02 a share, and because another $83 million to $85 million of issuance is slated to run through 2026, it is difficult to dismiss the suspicion that each incremental raise will keep chipping away at reported EPS. While fresh equity does spare the balance sheet some leverage, an ordinary investor, glancing at a brokerage screen rather than an infrastructure financing model, naturally focuses on the near-term dilution, and the tension between funding growth and protecting shareholder value becomes immediately palpable.

Dilution is only the first brick through the window of a company that once sold itself as utility royalty. In the same quarter, the pension portfolio coughed up a five-cent hit to earnings, erasing last year’s gain. Then came the rain-soaked slowdown at ASUS, shaving $1.8 million off revenue and turning a federal-contracting side into another line-item headache. One by one, these “one-offs” are lining up like a chain of bar tabs on the income statement. Management swears those weather-stalled dollars will show up later, but the mere fact that timing glitches can rattle a supposed bunker-grade stock shows just how much the earnings fog has rolled in.

What used to be a cozy regulatory breeze has turned into a coin-flip forecast. After dumping its old revenue-decoupling setup for a Monterey-style Water Revenue Adjustment Mechanism in January 2025, AWR’s California unit no longer enjoys the cushion that once muffled swings in consumption and supply costs. To be sure, first-quarter results benefitted fortuitously from a cheaper mix, more pumped water and less expensive purchased supply, but management acknowledged that the mix could easily tilt the other way in the next quarter. What nags is the sense that the California Public Utilities Commission, having already denied AWR’s request to preserve the old decoupling approach, may leave the company exposed to volume risk just as conservation efforts and climate volatility make demand forecasting trickier. Even if AWR prevails on rehearing and re-instates some of the prior protections, the episode hints that regulators are willing to ratchet up earnings variability at precisely the moment investors crave stability.

Operating costs keep climbing and not just because everything costs more: even after stripping out supply, AWR shelled out another $2 million year-over-year, much of it for wildfire and vegetation work that regulators now let the company wedge into the rate base, fine for recovery, brutal for the baseline. With labor and materials still priced like artisanal gold, any new uptick can pinch margins, especially under a rate formula that no longer cushions earnings from volume swings. Stack that slow burn on top of share dilution and the tidy utility narrative starts to unravel. Over in the federal-contracting shop, the ballast has slipped its moorings: no 50-year military privatization deals on deck for 2025, and the shorter 15-year versions demand more red tape for skinnier risk-adjusted payoffs, turning what once offset California volatility into another source of timing and execution headaches.

Put all that smoke-alarm spending and contract slippage next to a share count that keeps inching higher, and you finally have to look up at the scoreboard: price. Shares equate to 24.03× trailing earnings; by contrast, management’s own modeling, or at least consensus, pegs fair value at 15×, rising only to 20.93× by 2027.

Put differently, the market price is roughly 60% above the calculated fair value of $48.39, a gap that implies a growth trajectory far more vibrant than the numbers suggest.

Looking forward, EPS is expected to inch ahead 3.68% in 2025, 5.73% in 2026, and top out at 7.19% in 2027 before moderating again.

Those percentages, respectable for a regulated utility, nevertheless struggle to justify a forward P/E of 23.58, fully 34% higher than the sector median. Likewise, the PEG ratio of 4.17 versus the peer group’s 2.50 means investors are effectively paying almost twice as much per unit of growth.

All of this feeds into an estimated annual shareholder return of -8.87% (per FAST Graphs data above), a figure that hinges more on the sober math of multiple compression. The risk is not that the dividend, targeted to grow 8% annually, will vanish overnight; rather, the worry is that payout growth outstrips earnings expansion, forcing a choice between balance-sheet strain and a slower dividend trajectory. Profitability metrics already hint at pressure: EBITDA growth of 4.52% sits about 41% below the five-year average and trails the sector’s 8.82%, while EBIT growth is down nearly 60% from trend.

Revenue still advances at 6.79%, yet that top-line strength fails to flow through, suggesting either rising input costs or tariff structures that do not fully capture inflation.

Cash-flow trends, on the surface, look sparkling: operating cash flow surged 85.85% year over year and could rise another 47.23%. But context matters: capital expenditure is growing more than 34%, roughly quadruple the sector norm, raising the possibility that the working-capital tailwinds inflating cash-flow growth are transient. If so, free cash flow could end up thinner than headline figures imply, exactly when investors need proof that the dividend can be funded without leaning on incremental share sales or debt.

Final Takeaway

I’d label AWR a Hold because the shares already trade well above fair value, making fresh upside hard to pencil out, yet the company’s balance-sheet strength, multi-decade dividend record, and newly approved California rate base still offer a cushion against steep downside. Put simply, the stock looks too expensive to buy but too well capitalized, thanks to investment-grade credit, ample liquidity, and visible cap-ex recovery mechanisms, to dump outright. I’d wait for either a valuation pullback or clearer margin erosion before moving to a stronger stance.
